analogy: Think of "Growing up as Boys and Girls" through the chapter's own opening images: Growing up as Boys and Girls Being a boy or a girl is an important part of ones identity. The society we grow up in teaches us what kind of behaviour is acceptable for girls and boys, what boys and girls can or cannot do.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 4CHAPTER, 39 Chapter 4: Growing up as Boys and Girls, 40 Social and Political Life, MY MOTHER. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Growing up as Boys and Girls is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that growing up as Boys and Girls Being a boy or a girl is an important part of ones identity.

The text explains that the society we grow up in teaches us what kind of behaviour is acceptable for girls and boys, what boys and girls can or cannot do.

The text explains that we often grow up thinking that these things are exactly the same everywhere.

The text explains that but do all societies look at boys and girls in the same way?

The text explains that we will also look at how the different roles assigned to boys and girls prepare them for their future roles as men and women.

The text explains that we will learn that most societies value men and women differently.

The text explains that the roles women play and the work they do are usually valued less than the roles men play and the work they do.

The text explains that this chapter will also examine how inequalities between men and women emerge in the area of work.

The text explains that in what ways do the experiences of Samoan children and teenagers differ from your own experiences of growing up?

The text explains that is there anything in this experience that you wish was part of your growing up?

The text explains that a Class VII Samoan child in his school uniform.

The text explains that why do girls like to go to school together in groups?

The text explains that growing up in Samoa in the 1920s The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the Pacific Ocean.

The text explains that in the 1920s, according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school.

The text explains that they learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults.

The text explains that fishing was a very important activity on the islands.

The text explains that they had a central courtyard where they played in total seclusion and 39 Chapter 4: Growing up as Boys and Girls

The text explains that can you think of reasons why there are fewer women and girls in your neighbourhood streets, parks and markets in the late evenings or at night?

The text explains that can you think of reasons why this might be so?

The text explains that tasks like Chapter 4: Growing up as Boys and Girls

The text explains that it is part of a larger system of inequality between men and women.

The text explains that it, therefore, has to be dealt with through actions not just at the level of the individual or the family but also by the government.

The text explains that as we now know, equality is an important principle of our Constitution.

The text explains that the Constitution says that being male or female should not become a reason for discrimination.

The text explains that the government is, therefore, committed to understanding the reasons for this and taking positive steps to remedy the situation.

The text explains that the government has set up anganwadis or child-care centres in several villages in the country.

The text explains that the government has passed laws that make it mandatory for organisations that have more than 30 women employees to provide crèche facilities.

The text explains that chapter 4: Growing up as Boys and Girls Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) www.in.undp.org

The text explains that if there is a difference between the two lists, can you think of some reasons why this is so?

The text explains that our society does not make distinctions between boys and girls when they are growing up. c.

The text explains that glossary Identity: Identity is a sense of self-awareness of who one is.

The text explains that typically, a person can have several identities.

The text explains that for example, a person can be a girl, a sister and a musician.

The text explains that double-burden: Literally means a double load.

The text explains that this term is commonly used to describe the womens work situation.

The text explains that it has emerged from a recognition that women typically labour both inside the home (housework) and outside.

The text explains that care-giving: Care-giving refers to a range of tasks related to looking after and nurturing.

The text explains that besides physical tasks, they also involve a strong emotional aspect.

The text explains that de-valued: When someone is not given due recognition for a task or job they have done, they can feel de-valued.

The text explains that for example, if a boy has put in a lot of effort into making a special birthday gift for his friend and this friend does not say anything about this, then the boy may feel de-valued.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 4: Growing up as Boys and Girls

revision_bullets: Growing up as Boys and Girls Being a boy or a girl is an important part of ones identity. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
The society we grow up in teaches us what kind of behaviour is acceptable for girls and boys, what boys and girls can or cannot do. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
We often grow up thinking that these things are exactly the same everywhere. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
But do all societies look at boys and girls in the same way? (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
We will also look at how the different roles assigned to boys and girls prepare them for their future roles as men and women. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
We will learn that most societies value men and women differently. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
The roles women play and the work they do are usually valued less than the roles men play and the work they do. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
This chapter will also examine how inequalities between men and women emerge in the area of work. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
In what ways do the experiences of Samoan children and teenagers differ from your own experiences of growing up? (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)
Is there anything in this experience that you wish was part of your growing up? (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch4 pg 1)

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

As soon as babies could walk, their mothers or other adults no longer looked after them.

options: Older children, often as young as five years old, took over this responsibility.
Both boys and girls looked after their younger siblings.
But, by the time a boy was about nine years old, he joined the older boys in learning outdoor jobs like fishing and planting...
Girls had to continue looking after small children or do errands for adults till they were teenagers.
